name: Luna (agate area)
short_description: Free public BLM rockhounding for colorful agate and chalcedony nodules at 7045 feet elevation in remote Catron County. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Luna sits in the high desert of southwestern New Mexico's Catron County, offering excellent agate hunting on open BLM land. The area around Luna is known for scattered agate nodules weathering out of the surrounding volcanic terrain, with colors ranging from white and gray to banded varieties. This is classic Basin and Range geology where Tertiary-age volcanic rocks have fractured and weathered over millions of years, exposing chalcedony-filled cavities. The landscape is relatively flat at 7045 feet elevation with sparse pinyon-juniper vegetation, making walking and searching straightforward compared to steeper canyon areas nearby.

The agate nodules here typically range from walnut to grapefruit size and weather out of hillsides and washes. Many specimens show banding or interesting color patterns. This is a quiet, little-known spot that does not draw heavy collector pressure, so good material is still abundant. The nearest services are in the small town of Luna itself, but bring all water and supplies as facilities are extremely limited.

Because this is public BLM land, there are no fees, no permits, and no reservation needed. Just show up, park responsibly, and collect within the daily 25 lb limit. The flat terrain and easy walking make this suitable for families and beginning rockhounds. Winter weather can be cold at this elevation, but fall and spring are ideal for comfortable digging and collecting.
history: The agate nodules at Luna formed during the Tertiary period (roughly 30-60 million years ago) when widespread volcanic activity deposited silica-rich fluids throughout the Basin and Range province. These fluids filled cavities and fractures in host rocks; over time, silica precipitated as chalcedony, forming the agate nodules rockhounds find today. Weathering and erosion have exposed these nodules on hillsides and in washes, making surface collection possible.
tips: Search hillsides and gentle slopes where erosion has exposed darker nodules against lighter soil - the contrast makes them easy to spot. Use a classifier or small strainer to sift through gravelly wash material after rain, as water helps reveal fresh material. Look for rounded, translucent or banded stones with a waxy luster; discard solid rocks that lack color variation. Bring a 5 gallon bucket to haul your finds and ensure you stay under the 25 lb daily limit by stopping regularly to weigh progress.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7045 feet in open pinyon-juniper country with flat to gently rolling terrain. Sparse vegetation and good visibility make navigation straightforward. Most collecting is accessible by short walks of less than a mile from parking. Soil is rocky and compacted; footing is generally stable with no major obstacles. Afternoon thunderstorms are possible in summer months.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures and lower chance of afternoon thunderstorms common in summer. Winter can bring freezing conditions at this elevation; summer heat is moderate but dry.
written_directions: From Luna (the small community in Catron County), head north or northeast on local Forest Service roads and BLM access roads toward the agate fields. GPS coordinates 33.818918, -108.954836 mark the general area. Most access is via unpaved BLM roads passable by passenger vehicle in dry conditions; check current road status with the BLM Quemado Field Office before visiting. Cell service is spotty, so download offline maps and mark waypoints before heading out.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM roads, passable by passenger vehicle when dry. May become muddy or rough after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Pullouts and informal parking along BLM roads near the collecting area. No developed lot; park responsibly off the road.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose material; do not break rocks or dig into bedrock.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No breaking of rock outcrops
nearest_services: Luna (very small community), approximately 5 miles. Quemado has more services approximately 25 miles south. Socorro (larger town with all amenities) approximately 75 miles southeast.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved BLM road to parking area. Collecting areas are short walks from parking with stable footing on rocky soil. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy footwear recommended.

faq.question: What types of agate can I find rockhounding near Luna, New Mexico?
faq.answer: Luna is known for colorful banded agate and chalcedony nodules weathering from Tertiary volcanic rocks. Specimens often display white, gray, tan, and brown bands or patterns. Most nodules range from walnut to grapefruit size and have a waxy luster. You may also find solid chalcedony pieces and quartz.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect agate at Luna, New Mexico?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Luna is on public BLM land where recreational mineral collection is allowed under FLPMA regulations. Simply show up and collect within the 25 lb per day limit. There are no fees, reservations, or special paperwork.

faq.question: How much agate can I collect per day at the Luna agate area?
faq.answer: You can collect up to 25 lbs per day on this BLM land. This is the standard personal use limit under FLPMA. Bring a scale or weigh your finds periodically to stay within the limit.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for agate at Luna?
faq.answer: April - May and September - October are ideal. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures and lower risk of afternoon thunderstorms. Summer can be dry but is manageable; winter at 7045 feet elevation can be cold and occasionally snowy.

faq.question: Is the Luna agate area beginner-friendly and suitable for families with children?
faq.answer: Yes. The terrain is flat with easy access and no major obstacles. Short walks from parking reach productive collecting areas. There are no difficult scrambles or dangerous terrain, making it suitable for children ages 6 and up. Bring water, sun protection, and ensure someone carries a whistle or communicates your location to others due to remote setting.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring rockhounding at Luna, New Mexico?
faq.answer: Bring a 5 gallon bucket, small hand shovel, rock hammer, classifier or strainer for wash material, sturdy gloves, and a scale to weigh your 25 lb limit. Most importantly, bring at least 2-3 liters of water per person, sun hat, and sunscreen. A GPS device or offline map is essential since cell service is spotty in this remote area.

meta_title: Luna Agate Rockhounding - New Mexico BLM
meta_description: Free public BLM rockhounding for agate and chalcedony near Luna, New Mexico. No permit, 25 lb daily limit. Easy terrain at 7045 ft elevation.
